title: Architecture Walkthrough
description: How graph-browser works — from JSON data to interactive visualization.

stops.title: The Core Principle
stops.narrative:
[Data-Driven Design](node:data-driven) is the foundation of everything. The PureScript code contains zero domain-specific knowledge — all visual elements come from JSON data files.

This means a knowledge graph about [Cardano governance](https://github.com/lambdasistemi/cardano-governance-graph), [local LLMs](https://github.com/lambdasistemi/local-llm-graph), or the graph-browser's own architecture all use the same code. You provide data, it provides visualization.

stops.title: The Central Component
stops.narrative:
The [Viewer](node:viewer) is where everything happens. It's a [Halogen](node:halogen) component that manages the graph canvas, search box, sidebar, controls, and tutorial navigation.

Viewer holds the full application state: which node is selected, what's hovered, search results, tutorial progress, and the prompt builder input. All user interactions — clicking nodes, changing depth, searching — flow through Viewer's action handlers.

stops.title: Two Entry Points
stops.narrative:
Graph-browser has two entry points that both use [Viewer](node:viewer).

[Main](node:main) is the app variant — it adds a [RepoManager](node:repo-manager) panel on the left for browsing multiple repos. It handles deep linking via ?repo= URLs and persists the repo list.

[Lib](node:lib) is the standalone variant — it mounts Viewer directly with hardcoded data paths. Downstream repos consume this via Nix flake input.

stops.title: The Rendering Layer
stops.narrative:
[FFI.Cytoscape](node:ffi-cytoscape) is the bridge between PureScript and [Cytoscape.js](node:cytoscape-js). It's a thin JavaScript binding that initializes the graph canvas, sets elements, handles layout, and subscribes to interaction events.

The fCoSE layout algorithm adapts to graph size — smaller graphs get more spacing, larger graphs pack tighter. Node colors and shapes come from config.json kinds, mapped through the FFI layer.

stops.title: Depth-Based Filtering
stops.narrative:
[Depth-based filtering](node:depth-filtering) is the key UX innovation. Instead of overwhelming users with the entire graph, the viewer shows a neighborhood around the selected node.

Depth 1 shows immediate neighbors. Depth 2 shows two hops. The BFS algorithm in [Graph.Operations](node:graph-operations) uses adjacency maps for efficient traversal. This is how a 70-node graph remains navigable.

stops.title: The Build System
stops.narrative:
The [Nix Flake](node:nix-flake) orchestrates the entire build. It invokes [esbuild](node:esbuild) to bundle npm dependencies (Cytoscape.js + fCoSE), then [Spago](node:spago) to compile PureScript, then concatenates the outputs.

Two build outputs: [App](node:app-output) (hosted universal viewer) and [Lib](node:lib-output) (embeddable for downstream repos). Both produced in the Nix sandbox with zero network access.

stops.title: Schema Validation
stops.narrative:
The [reusable CI workflow](node:validate-workflow) lets data repos validate their JSON with one line of GitHub Actions config.

It fetches the [graph](node:schema-graph), [config](node:schema-config), and [tutorial](node:schema-tutorial) schemas, validates with ajv-cli, then checks edge integrity, kind references, and tutorial node references. This very graph is validated by this very workflow — dogfooding at its finest.

stops.title: LLM Integration
stops.narrative:
The [PromptBuilder](node:prompt-builder) closes the loop. Click any node, type what you want an LLM to do, and get a complete prompt with all the context: the node's JSON, connected edges, [schema](node:schema-graph) URLs, repo URL, and PR instructions.

The LLM gets everything it needs to propose valid changes. The user provides intent; the tool provides context and process.
